data.title: Chevron: Growth Drivers Are In Sync With Valuation And Technicals
data.description:
Oil and gas stocks have become more interesting lately amid the intense situation in the Middle East. Chevron Corporation (CVX) greatly benefited from it in line with the rising prices. On top of that, I believe the recent rally was justifiable considering my conservative target price, or TP, of $200.73 in my previous coverage. At its current price, CVX may have already priced in everything at a discount. Technicals adhere to valuation and fundamentals as bullish momentum remains evident despite the recent overbuying and profit-taking.

Iran War: I Think Chevron Benefits From It

Oil prices have skyrocketed to their two-year highs as the Middle East tension escalated. I believe that CVX greatly benefited from it since higher oil prices mean higher oil production margins. After all, it is an upstream-heavy company. It’s not surprising that oil prices can easily affect its earnings and stock price. That is why the US-Iran ceasefire led to oil price drops, which also pushed CVX down on a single day. In addition, I believe that the impact of the ceasefire will affect CVX more than Exxon Mobil (XOM), since CVX is more upstream-heavy compared to XOM.

On a lighter note, the positive impact of the deal will be more on its production stability. I also believe that the actual impact of the recent tension has remained manageable for CVX. Why? CVX had already expected to cut its oil production by 185,000 to 225,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day even before the war started. So, war-related oil production cuts (if it really happened to CVX) were probably priced in already. And I think this is what makes it a bit different from XOM.

Moreover, CVX may benefit from the reopening of its Leviathan gas production in offshore Israel. This is a crucial aspect since it helps CVX diversify its presence outside the Americas. The production capacity here is also high. Currently, it has around 12 billion cubic meters per year. This is targeted to be nearly doubled to 21 BCM per year. Aside from that, CVX may receive more demand since gas is crucial to ensure energy security, especially in Israel and Jordan. These are two nations directly involved in /affected by the tension. Aside from that, the ceasefire does not automatically mean that the tension between Israel and Iran is also over. Note that Israel attacked Lebanon and killed at least 254 people there just after the US and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire. The tension may still escalate despite the ceasefire. In addition, supply shocks have not really materialized yet. Of course, we hope not to reach this point since many countries still have oil reserves, which I think can cover 30-60 days. But we must remember that the war has already led to shutdowns and damage in oil, gas, and other energy facilities and fields in the region. So, I believe in three things. One is that the oil price drop is not conclusive. Another is that oil prices may not normalize to pre-war levels anytime soon. Third, CVX’s Leviathan operations may be in demand this quarter and drive stable cash flow in the long run. In the succeeding years, its demand may be spurred by the global data center expansion. Overall, I believe that the net impact of the tension and the reopening of the Leviathan operations on CVX is still positive.

It Remains Sustainable

Amid market volatility, I believe it’s more important to highlight how capable CVX is of sustaining its huge and capital-intensive operations and dividends. If you look at its most recent Balance Sheet, its cash and cash equivalents remain ample at $6.2B. This should not be surprising since its operating cash inflow remains enough to cover its increasing capex. Its operating cash inflow also remained enough to cover the acquisition of Hess Corporation. By doing so, CVX obtained a substantial stake in Guyana. This can be a low-cost oil production area for CVX since Guyana is still a young oil source. Exploration, drilling, and production may be easier and cheaper than in mature basins and wells.

CVX complements it with prudent debt management through strategic and timely issuances and repayments to stay liquid. In fact, it maintains a decent maturity profile because 98% of the total will not mature within the year. To be more specific, 66% of the total has a maturity of four years and longer. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of only 0.9x, which means that CVX only needs 0.9 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. This proves it keeps enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

At its current price, CVX has already risen by roughly 11%. It even reached $211 before selling pressures increased and pushed the price down. It’s also worth noting that it has already reached its resistance and retraced to $190-195. And I think that makes sense if we relate it to my Dividend Discount Model TP before. Considering this, I believe it’s wise to reassess it again.

The annualized dividend is now $7.12 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.78. This leads to a dividend yield of 3.53%, which is notably higher than that of Exxon Mobil’s (XOM) 2.51%. Also, the dividend growth rate is higher at 6.01% based on its five-year growth. Meanwhile, I lowered its risk premium to 6.5% because I realized that 7.0% is already too high and too conservative. After all, the market standard is only 5.0%. So, I believe that it prices in enough risk factors. Lastly, Beta is 0.66, which means that CVX has become less volatile than in my previous coverage. This also means that its price swings are more stable than the S&P 500. More importantly, its Beta is more than twice the Beta of XOM at only 0.28. This should not be surprising since CVX is much more upstream-heavy than XOM. So, its earnings are typically more sensitive to oil price swings. This is the reason why its risk premium is also higher than XOM at only 5.5%.

With all this information, my new TP is $266.45. This shows that the stock is still discounted. Even if I add a 10% risk consideration, the TP will still be higher at $239.80. I also made a model where I retained the 7.0% risk premium. Even so, we can still prove the reasonability of the stock price. This leads to a TP of $237.21 and $213.49 after the 10% risk consideration. Hence, CVX is still trading at a discount.

Technicals

CVX’s technicals are still bullish as the stock price increases with higher highs and lows. We have seen selling pressures in the past week after the resistance, but that makes sense in line with the cautious stance of some investors. After all, it has already reached its one-year high. It is also trending above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs even after the notable price drop yesterday, which suggests that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which suggests that market sentiments are still generally optimistic.

Additionally, MACD and RSI remain in line with stock price. You can see that their highs from February 12 to March 30 were still consistent with the price highs. With that, we cannot detect any bearish divergence yet. But of course, they still signal caution. The MACD line is decreasing below the signal line amid the increasing selling volume. The histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. Despite this, it’s nice to know that CVX's closing price was higher than the opening. This means that many investors would not accept any price below $190. If you look at the wick of the candlestick yesterday, you can see that sellers attempted to pull the price to $188 but were unsuccessful, as buyers took this as an opportunity to enter at a lower price. This should not be surprising since it has been two or three weeks since the price has broken above $200. Lastly, RSI shows that the dip was reasonable because the stock had already become overbought, which prompted profit-taking. This was amplified by the news about the ceasefire, which also pushed oil prices below $100 per barrel yesterday. But this also means that there is wider buying room today.

Conclusion

Chevron Corporation stays well-positioned amid the intense situation in the Middle East. Its robust liquidity, strategic geographical diversification, well-diversified business model, and the reopening of its Leviathan gas production are some factors to support its resilience and upside. More importantly, its valuation remains low with an attractive upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as bullish momentum remains evident, and buyers took advantage of the price drop yesterday. I’m reiterating my strong buy rating on this stock.

data.title: SiTime's Renesas Deal Makes This Stock Harder To Buy
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Jamelle Danne Rosales as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

There are companies you follow for years waiting for the story to click into place, and then there are companies where everything seems to click at once, and suddenly you have to decide whether the moment you've been waiting for is actually the right time to act.

SiTime Corporation (SITM) is one of those companies right now.

If you're not familiar with it, the short version is this:

SiTime makes the tiny timing semiconductors that sit inside virtually every piece of modern electronics and tell it when to do what.

It's a niche that sounds mundane until you realize that AI data centers, 5G networks, and high-speed communications infrastructure all run on timing precision (and that SiTime has been quietly winning that market by replacing decades-old quartz technology with something better).

The company has been building toward this moment for years. Revenue has nearly doubled in two years. Margins are expanding. Customers are some of the most demanding in the industry.

Then, on February 4, 2026, SiTime reported one of the cleanest quarterly beats in its history and (in the same press release) announced a $1.5 billion acquisition of Renesas' timing business financed in part with $900 million in new debt.

So, naturally, the stock popped to an all-time high and then gave back 23% over the next seven weeks.

That kind of reaction tends to mean the market is genuinely uncertain about something. What follows is my attempt to work through both of those stories honestly where the evidence is strong. Where it requires more proof, and what I think the right posture is for investors trying to figure out what to do with SITM at roughly $345 a share.

Investment Thesis

I want to be clear about my position. SiTime is a truly remarkable company, and I have been following it long enough to see how the narrative has refined itself in the last two years.

The rate of revenue growth and margins are increasing, plus, management has been able to support its own public announcements with real performance.

The bull case is not difficult to make on the organic evidence alone.

However on February 4, 2026, SiTime declared a $1.5 billion cash acquisition of Renesas timing business (funded in part by 900 million new debt) and in the process, essentially transformed the nature of the investment.

The company that used to be clean and debt-free transformed into leveraged and integration-dependent within a single afternoon.

This does not necessarily turn the stock into a Sell. It does, to me, render it a materially more complicated Hold than it was the day before.

The Renesas acquisition could eventually become a landmark strategic step that hastens SiTime to the $1 billion revenue mark and establishes it as the leading pure-play precision timing platform.

Or it can turn out to be a peak-cycle acquisition that burdened a semiconductor company that had been fast, lean, with a 30-year-old, 10,000-customer legacy business it was not yet equipped to absorb.

The stock at about $345 (and is not obviously cheap). Neither is it obviously costly. What it is, I believe, is a case in which the quality of the business and the complexity of the transition have become difficult to decouple, and until the economics of the acquisition start to show themselves in the actual performance, I would prefer to wait and prepare than to commit and regret.

Business Model And Industry Context

SiTime's core proposition is straightforward enough that it doesn't need excessive elaboration. It designs MEMS-based oscillators and timing semiconductors that replace legacy quartz crystals (the tiny frequency references inside virtually every electronic device).

Quartz has served well for decades, but it has limitations in the applications where precision and resilience actually matter. It is not easily programmable, it struggles at temperature extremes, and it does not tolerate vibration particularly well.

MEMS silicon addresses all of those limitations (and is fundamentally more flexible in a world where equipment designers want programmable, supply-chain-simplifying components). The market is also enormous (estimates range from $5.5-$10 billion globally) and quartz still controls roughly 95–96% of it. SiTime holds approximately 90% of the MEMS slice, which currently represents only 4–5% of the total.

The long-term thesis that is true whether or not there is cyclical dynamics of the near term is the displacement story to me. Although AI CapEx may slow down, the structural benefits of MEMS timing relative to quartz are not eliminated. And I think the addressable runway (in dollar terms) is mostly unaddressed.

I think the short-term boost is attributable to the fact that AI data centers are constructed left and right, where latency isn't a quality-of-life thing, it's what separates a company from being or not being chosen, and the more computers you have the more they should coordinate.

SiTime's TimeFabric software suite, launched in June 2025 with claims of nine times better synchronization accuracy than quartz equivalents, is the company's first serious move toward a systems-level positioning.

It is early, and its financial contribution is not yet separately reported, but it signals something important about where management sees the company going less "component supplier" and more of a "timing infrastructure platform."

What The Latest Developments Actually Showed

The Q4 2025 numbers as clean of a beat as you can get in the semiconductor industry. Revenue of 113.3 million was received 11% higher than consensus of 101.9 million and 66% higher than the previous year. Non-GAAP EPS of $1.53 was triple Q4 2024's $0.48.

Non-GAAP gross margin reached 61.2% (surpassing 60% that management had publicly promised at the beginning of 2025). Revenue grew 61% to $326.7 million in its entire fiscal year, against a figure of $202.7 million in FY2024 and about $143.7 million in FY2023.

That progression matters. I don't think it's a one-quarter-story. It is three years of consecutive improvement, which is faster and not slowing growth rate. Communications, Enterprise, and Datacenter segment [CED] has seven consecutive quarters of more than 100% year-over-year growth. CED has become about 53% of total 2025 revenue and the driver of the whole mix-shift thesis.

Q1 2026 estimates of $101-$104 million would mean about 70% growth per annum, compared to about 14% previous analyst expectations of $91 million.

The deal buys a business with revenue of around $300 million in the year one, gross margin of about 70%, and connections with the top 10 cloud hyperscalers, the top 7 AI server makers and the top 10 enterprise networking suppliers at a cost of $1.5 billion in cash and 4.13 million shares of SITM stock.

The company declared it a landmark step toward the establishment of the leading precision timing company and ensured non-GAAP EPS accretion in the first year. I believe that pullback is the market posing a question that the initial announcement failed to resolve in all its extent:

Is this acquisition really transformational?

Why It Matters

Before February 4, SiTime's investment profile was about as uncomplicated as a high-growth semiconductor story can be. Debt-free. $808 million in cash. Fabless, capital-light, and organically compounding against a clear structural tailwind.

After February 4, it's a different company. Not necessarily worse, but different in a way that we have to look into it deeply. The $900 million in new debt introduces financial risk the balance sheet previously didn't have.

The 4.13 million new shares represent roughly 15% dilution to existing shareholders. The acquired business (a 30-year-old timing franchise from a company by the way) for its own stated strategic reasons, deciding to exit this market, needs to be integrated, rationalized, and grown by a management team with no acquisition track record at this scale.

These are the natural consequences of the transaction structure SiTime chose.

I found it honest to say this:

The deal makes strategic sense in a general way. Adding clocking, buffering, and synchronization products (overall increasing SiTime's portfolio by more than tenfold) while simultaneously inheriting established positions at every major hyperscaler is not something you can build organically in two years.

The Renesas MOU to potentially integrate MEMS resonators into Renesas MCUs and SoCs is an optionality element no one is currently modeling. These are genuinely compelling parts of the thesis.

But the strategic logic and the execution reality are two different things. And the one piece of the research that I think is conspicuously absent from most bullish coverage of SITM deserves a mention here:

Apple is SiTime's largest disclosed end customer per the 10-K filing. Consumer and mobile applications are still approximately 47% of SiTime's own 2025 revenue.

The company's AI datacenter transformation story is real (BUT it has not eliminated meaningful exposure to a consumer technology buyer who makes its own silicon decisions on its own timeline) for its own reasons. That concentration risk does not appear to be priced into anything. That bothers me more than a little, but we must carry on with the bull and bear cases below:

The Bull Case

I want to give the bull case its full due, because I think the dismissive take on SiTime misses something real.

Start with the base rate. Revenue grew 41% in FY2024, then accelerated to 61% in FY2025. Q4 2025 beat consensus by 11%. Q1 2026 guidance came in 14% above the prior analyst consensus.

In semiconductors, this kind of sustained acceleration across multiple reported and audited quarters is evidence of something structural. Management set a specific, public target at the start of 2025:

However, it is not the same company. The purchase altered the math and valuation of things since the bar to execute this has been raised considerably higher.

To hold this stock with real confidence, you must believe all of the things that you see happen to the Renesas integration:

Everything should be clean, that you get to see the acquired revenue to the tune of $300 million dollars, that the company gets to keep it, the company grows it, deleverage, CED organically grows without an AI spending freeze, and that you also get the Apple related exposure to become the headline risk.

Such a list of things to get right at once, at a valuation that is already discounting most of the good news.

Combined, the path to $1 billion in revenue is not too far as everyone thinks. At an organic run-rate approaching $450 million annualized, plus $300 million acquired, you are at $750 million before applying any growth. Management's $1 billion target requires only modest further acceleration.

The customer inheritance may be the single most undervalued element of the deal. SiTime's organic [CED] growth is real, but winning positions at the top 10 cloud hyperscalers and the top 7 AI server manufacturers through organic sales cycles would have taken years and significant investment.

And then there is the [MOU] that essentially no one is modeling. The intent to integrate SiTime's MEMS resonators into Renesas' embedded computing products (MCUs and SoCs, which ship in hundreds of millions of units annually) is an optionality element that could be material at scale. It is speculative, yes. But it is a genuine free option that exists and has a logical commercial mechanism. Investors who get in before it develops are buying it at zero.

Sell-side consensus sits at roughly seven or eight Buy ratings with a median price target of $442–$450 versus a current price around $315. Even accounting for the inherent optimism in sell-side targets, a 27–30% implied upside is not nothing. And the same community that historically underestimated SiTime's organic growth is now uniformly constructive. That is worth noting, even if it is not sufficient on its own.

The Bear Case And Key Risks

I want to be equally honest about the other side, because I think parts of the bear case are underappreciated in the current conversation.

Start with the acquisition terms. SiTime paid approximately $1.5 billion in cash plus 4.13 million shares, total consideration potentially approaching $3 billion, for $300 million in year-one revenue.

That implies roughly 10 times forward sales for a business whose seller, Renesas, is exiting for stated strategic focus reasons. Renesas is a sophisticated operator.

The question I keep coming back to is: why now, at this price, in this market? I am not claiming the deal is wrong.

I am saying the acquisition logic is not as one-sided as the SiTime narrative implies, and a thoughtful investor should sit with that asymmetry for a moment.

The leverage transformation is real and significant. $808 million in cash and zero debt gives them more room for expenditures without sacrificing heavily their short-term liquidity.

The ability to invest counter-cyclically, to weather a demand downturn without financial stress, to attract and retain talent in a competitive market. That flexibility is now gone. What replaces it is roughly $900 million in bridge debt that needs to be refinanced into permanent capital at whatever interest rates that structure requires.

Post-deal interest expense becomes a real earnings headwind. If the AI capex cycle softens in 2027, SiTime faces that environment with far less cushion than it has had for most of its public market life.

The Apple Inc. (AAPL) issue is the one I keep coming back to in the context of the overall risk assessment, because it is so consistently absent from bullish coverage. The 10-K names Apple as the largest end customer. Consumer and mobile applications are approximately 47% of SiTime's own 2025 revenue, the part that is not [CED].

Apple's procurement decisions, its silicon integration roadmap, and its supply chain strategy are entirely outside SiTime's control, but I am not saying Apple will cut SiTime tomorrow. What I’m trying to say is that this is a material risk the market isn't really paying attention to.

Another thing I'm skeptical of is how the market reacted to the Q4 beat, I know Seeking Alpha favors fundamental analysis over technical analysis, but from a technical analysis standpoint, the stock seems like it’s moving by itself.

It pulled back 23% from its February highs despite a genuine Q4 beat, and sure we can all argue that most of this is attributable to macro trends and the market in general. But I think some of it is the institutions reaching to their own conclusions about what the acquisition does to the risk/reward profile. You see, there's a reason why it pulled back and it's the same reason why I'm issuing a Hold.

Valuation Discussion

The main reason why I'm giving this a Hold is the fact that SiTime earned $3.20 per share on an adjusted basis and we're paying about 108x that to own one share. Even if the company doubles its earnings next year (which I think is still possible but not probable) the stock would still be trading at more than 50x of that.

And I think that is still expensive compared to most chip companies, but again, I don't necessarily think it's a bad investment, it just means there is very little room for error for a lot of things to go right.

Expectations for Renesas

After SiTime just agreed to buy Renesas for $1.5 billion, it's to bring in around $300 million in new revenue in its first year, with very strong profit margins of about 70%. Combine that with SiTime's own growing revenue, the company could be generating somewhere between $750 million and $800 million in total annual revenue and at a $9 billion market value, that works out to roughly 11 to 12x forward revenue, which I think is actually a reasonable price for a fast growing, high margin technology company. I think the main catch is that this only works if the deal closes smoothly and SiTime keeps all of that acquired revenue. Here are the three scenarios I see where price is headed for SiTime:

If things go wrong (Bear Case): $240 to $260

If the Renesas integration misses its targets, or if AI spending cools into 2027, or if Apple demand softens even slightly, the downside will directly impact share price. And the balance sheet is going to be an issue because $900 million in new debt means they'll be paying interest on that, which also means that the longer they don't deliver results, the longer they'll be paying interest on something that might not be of value as they thought it to be. So the longer it takes to prove the deal works, the more that interest becomes a real drag and that's going to be reflected in their share price.

If things stay uncertain (Base Case/Hold): $345 to $380

I think this is where we are now because the stock has already priced in a good portion of what the Renesas deal could become, but not enough has shown up in the actual numbers yet to justify pushing it meaningfully higher. Usually when companies acquire or invest in something (like Renesas), there's naturally going to be a gap between expectations and actual proof.

When you simply take a stroll through the figures, it begins to make a bit more sense why the stock is moving sideways. SiTime made approximately $326.7 million revenue last year and with the deal, you're looking at potential $600 to $650 million business now, but again, you are still paying about 13-14x forward revenue against a valuation of approximately $9 billion.

However, when things are good and revenue increases to, say, of $750 million, that multiple decreases towards 12x. And frankly, that is not far-fetched for a company that is expanding at such a rate with good margins, I believe SiTime is a good business and can pull it off, which is why I assume and current valuation is reflects that.

If things go right (Bull Case): $450 to $480

SiTime has reached approximately $326.7 million in revenue and with the Renesas acquisition, maybe another $300 million dollars. With further organic growth you can realistically expect to reach about $750-$800 million on a pro forma basis, and I'm basing this assumption with bad market conditions, if AI spending doesn't slow down (which I don't think it is going to slow down), it could be even more, but I'll keep those figures to keep things conservative.

If we assume margins remains in the 65%-70% range (which I believe is quite achievable based on their current trend), earnings power will increase rapidly. And when that occurs, the present $9 billion valuation will appear more realistic, which is like 11-12x forward revenue.

Assuming that happens, even a reversion to the 13-14x revenue on a bigger base takes you to an approximate of an $11-12 billion valuation, or about $450-$480 per share, about 30%-40% of upside with the current price ($345), which is, frankly, reasonable, as long as the company simply does its job as it is supposed to do it.

What To Watch Next

Q1 2026 stands out as the first place to watch out for in SiTime. Management has guided for $101–$104 million in revenue, and after an 11% beat last quarter, it’s fair to say expectations are elevated. If there’s a miss, saying they have a weak quarter isn’t enough, I’d probably want to understand if Q4 demand was pulled forward.

On the other hand, a beat and a guidance raise would reinforce the idea that AI-driven demand is still strong, plus if margins continue to move closer to 62%, I think it's a healthy sign that the company’s mix shift is progressing as planned.

Another issue is that Renesas needs to show its value as well, because it seems like the market expects the Renesas acquisition to go through without any problems, but that’s a very big assumption. If there are delays, regulatory issues, or complications in the U.S. or Japan, I’d expect the stock to react (and whether it goes up or down depends on that deal). And the same goes for the debt, $900 million is a lot, and if they refinance it smoothly, there’s no problem, but if the terms are or costly, SiTime's financials will weaken.

Risks to Consider

Execution around the Renesas acquisition is a significant risk here. SiTime used to be the clean, debt-free, and capital-light story, but this transaction adds $900 million worth of debt, potential share dilution, and a difficult integration process altogether. The acquisition won't pay off if the acquired revenues prove to be less than expected $300 million in the first year, or if the integration takes more time, or if refinancing comes on worse terms than expected by investors, resulting in the deteriorating financial case supporting the current valuation.

Another risk here is SiTime's exposure to both customers and end market concentration while the focus on AI/datacenters is growing stronger. Apple is still the leading disclosed customer and consumer/mobility segment represents a significant part of its revenues outside the rapidly growing CED market segment. It simply means that SiTime may become vulnerable to changes in customers' purchasing behavior and silicon strategy in consumer electronics despite expectations of seamless execution on the recently announced acquisition.

Only in case when SiTime will continue delivering organic growth, keeping its acquired revenue stream intact, reporting solid margins, and executing successful debt refinancing would my investment recommendation change in a positive way.

Conclusion

I think SiTime is a genuinely excellent business, especially with regulations shifting production of chips domestically, they're well-positioned.Revenue growth is exceptional.

Margin expansion is real and management delivered, not model assumed. The structural case for MEMS technology displacing quartz across AI infrastructure is supported by multiple quarters of audited evidence. If this were the same company it was on February 3, I would probably be writing a very different article.

To hold this stock with real confidence, you must believe all of the things that you see happen to the Renesas integration:

Everything should be clean, that you get to see the acquired revenue to the tune of $300 million dollars, that the company gets to keep it, the company grows it, deleverage, CED organically grows without an AI spending freeze, and that you also get the Apple related exposure to become the headline risk.

Such a list of things to get right at once, at a valuation that is already discounting most of the good news. My Hold is a considered opinion that the two quarters of reported evidence that will follow, Q1 2026 results, acquisition regulatory developments, and bridge refinancing conditions, will be more significant to this thesis than anything else in the future.

I would like to have that evidence before paying 108x non-GAAP earnings for a stock with a beta of 2.6 and a balance sheet that was clean (one day) and leveraged overnight the next.

In case Q1 can provide a clean beat and raise, regulatory approvals are achieved without hassle, and the bridge loan refinancing is raised at sensible rates, I would proceed to Buy without delay.

data.title: Corporate Profits Are Very Healthy
data.description:
Corporate profits are the mother's milk for equity prices, and they are stronger than ever relative to the size of the economy. No wonder the stock market has done so well in recent decades.

Chart #1

Chart #1 compares corporate profits (adjusted, and ex-Fed profits) to nominal GDP. According to the Q4/25 GDP estimates released today, corporate profits at the end of last year were up 8.4% from a year ago, and they totaled $3.6 trillion at an annualized pace.

Chart #2

Chart #2 shows the ratio of corporate profits to nominal GDP. As of the end of last year, profits were a record-setting 11.5% of GDP. Wow. Just wow. And it's not just a recent phenomenon. As the chart also shows, relative to the size of the economy, profits in recent years have been running twice as strong as they were in the 80s and early 90s.

Chart #3

Chart #3 shows the long-term path of the S&P 500 index as compared to an 8% annualized trend. When you add dividend yields of 1-2% per year, buying and holding stock in the country's 500 largest and most successful corporations has yielded about 10% per year since 1950.

Of course, there are times when returns have been far less than 10% per year and far greater. If you had bought stocks in October 2000, you wouldn't have broken even for almost 7 years.

In contrast, buying stocks in April 2009 (at the bottom of the Great Financial Crisis) would have delivered annualized returns of almost 15% plus dividends. It was several months early when, in November 2008, I argued that investing in stocks was the buying opportunity of a lifetime.

In any event, these three charts suggest that stocks today are neither very cheap nor very expensive from a historical perspective.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
